currentUse: Active Catholic church — the National Shrine of St. Alphonsus Liguori, a personal parish of the Archdiocese of Baltimore administered by the Priestly Fraternity of St. Peter (FSSP) as the archdiocese's sole Traditional Latin Mass site; no Lithuanian-language Mass is offered.
summary: Baltimore's Lithuanian parish was St. Alphonsus at 114 W. Saratoga Street, a Gothic Revival church built 1842-1845 for the German community and taken over by Lithuanians in 1917; the surrounding neighborhood became known as "Little Lithuania." The building stands and was never sold: it remains an archdiocesan church, listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1973 and designated a shrine in the 1990s. Its life as a Lithuanian parish effectively ended in 2017, when longtime pastor Msgr. Arthur Bastress retired and Archbishop William Lori entrusted the shrine to the Priestly Fraternity of St. Peter (administrator installed August 1, 2017); at that point the remaining 8:30 a.m. Sunday Mass for the Lithuanian community was celebrated by a non-Lithuanian archdiocesan priest, and the Lithuanian heritage site Global True Lithuania records the Lithuanian Mass as ended in 2017. In the archdiocese's 2024 "Seek the City to Come" restructuring — which cut Baltimore's city parishes by roughly two-thirds — St. Alphonsus survived and was strengthened: effective August 1, 2024 it was designated a personal parish without territorial boundaries and became the only site in the Archdiocese of Baltimore where the 1962 Missal Latin Mass is celebrated, under the FSSP. As of mid-2026 the parish website lists Fr. Ian Bozant, FSSP as pastor with three Sunday Latin Masses (7:30, 9:30, 11:30 a.m.) and daily Mass, but no Lithuanian liturgy. Lithuanian traces remain inside the church, including a "Siberian rosary" made by Lithuanian deportees, a Lithuanian stained-glass window, the tricolor flag, and a WWII memorial to Baltimore Lithuanian servicemen.

developments.headline: Shrine entrusted to the FSSP after retirement of Msgr. Bastress
developments.detail: Following Msgr. Arthur Bastress's retirement after nearly two decades as pastor, Archbishop William E. Lori entrusted the National Shrine of St. Alphonsus to the Priestly Fraternity of St. Peter; Fr. Joel E. Kiefer began as administrator August 1, 2017. At the time, archdiocesan priest Fr. William Spacek (not Lithuanian) celebrated an 8:30 a.m. Sunday Mass for the Lithuanian community — the 'token' Mass noted in the archive record.

sources.title: St. Alphonsus Shrine entrusted to Priestly Fraternity of St. Peter

developments.headline: Lithuanian-language Mass discontinued
developments.detail: The Lithuanian heritage documentation project Global True Lithuania records that the Lithuanian Mass at St. Alphonsus was canceled in 2017, leaving only English and Latin (Tridentine) liturgies. Lithuanian artifacts remain in the church: a Siberian rosary made by deportees, a Lithuanian stained-glass window, the Lithuanian tricolor, and a WWII memorial listing Baltimore Lithuanians who served.

sources.title: Baltimore, Maryland: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Survives 'Seek the City' restructuring; designated Latin Mass personal parish
developments.detail: In the Archdiocese of Baltimore's 'Seek the City to Come' consolidation (announced May 2024), which reduced city parishes from about 61 to the twenties, St. Alphonsus was not closed — instead, effective August 1, 2024 all celebration of the 1962 Missal in the archdiocese was consolidated there under the FSSP, and St. Alphonsus was designated a personal parish without territorial boundaries.

sources.title: Latin Mass to have permanent home at St. Alphonsus; will end at Hagerstown parish in August
Baltimore archdiocese proposes cutting 61 parishes to 21 in the city

developments.headline: Active FSSP shrine with daily Latin Mass; no Lithuanian liturgy listed
developments.detail: The parish website lists Fr. Ian Bozant, FSSP as pastor and Fr. Curtis Zuckerman, FSSP as parochial vicar, with Sunday Traditional Latin Masses at 7:30, 9:30, and 11:30 a.m. and weekday Masses. No Lithuanian Mass appears on the schedule.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: The archive record's status "closed / diocese_closed" needs refinement: the parish was never canonically suppressed and the building was never sold or closed. What ended was its Lithuanian identity — the archive's note of a "token Sunday Lithuanian Mass with no Lithuanian priest" matches the situation documented in September 2017, when archdiocesan (non-Lithuanian) priest Fr. William Spacek celebrated an 8:30 a.m. Mass for the Lithuanian community. That Mass has since been discontinued (Global True Lithuania dates the cancellation to 2017). The archive's "building used by an American parish" is confirmed and sharpened: since August 2017 the church has been administered by the FSSP as a Traditional Latin Mass apostolate, and since August 1, 2024 it is the archdiocese's designated Latin Mass personal parish. So the trajectory is transition/absorption within the diocese, not closure or sale — ownership remains diocese_rc throughout, consistent with the archive.
gaps: The archive record is "unnamed"; identification as St. Alphonsus (114 W. Saratoga St.) rests on the match between the archive notes (token Lithuanian Mass, no Lithuanian priest, building used by an American/non-Lithuanian congregation) and independently documented facts — the fit is strong and St. Alphonsus was Baltimore's only remaining Lithuanian parish, but the identification is inferred, not stated in the archive. Not found: an exact date or diocesan announcement for the final Lithuanian Mass (only Global True Lithuania's "2017"); any published record of where the Lithuanian congregation's artifacts or records would go if disposition ever occurs; any recent (2024-2026) press on Lithuanian community reunions or activity at the shrine specifically (Baltimore Lithuanian community life continues at the separate, non-parish Lithuanian Hall, not researched here). A search-result snippet claimed the earlier St. John the Baptist Lithuanian church closed in 1989, but this could not be verified on an opened page and conflicts with Global True Lithuania (which says Lithuanians used it only 1905-1916), so it is excluded from findings.
